era_context: The early 14th century CE spans the late Medieval period in Europe, the height of the Mali Empire in West Africa, the Yuan Dynasty in China under Mongol rule, and the Ottoman Empire's early expansion in Anatolia. This is an era of long-distance trade (Silk Road, trans-Saharan routes), religious ferment (Islamic expansion, Christian Crusades waning, Avignon Papacy), and significant technological diffusion (gunpowder weapons spreading westward, maritime innovations in the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Mali (limited written sources; oral traditions underrepresented)
Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited contemporary records)

events.title: Mansa Musa's Hajj to Mecca
events.region: Mali Empire, West Africa; Egypt; Arabia
events.description: Mansa Musa I of Mali undertook a pilgrimage to Mecca with a caravan of approximately 60,000 people, including 12,000 slaves carrying gold. The journey lasted several months and passed through Cairo, where Musa's lavish spending reportedly caused significant inflation in the Egyptian economy. This hajj demonstrated Mali's wealth and Islamic piety to the wider Muslim world and established Mali as a major player in trans-Saharan and Indian Ocean trade networks. The expedition was documented by contemporary Egyptian chroniclers and later by Ibn Battuta.
key_figures: Caliph al-Nasir Muhammad (Mamluk Sultan of Egypt)

sources.name: Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar fi mamalik al-amsar (Routes of Sight in the Kingdoms of the Lands)
Ibn Battuta, Rihla (The Journey) — written later but based on contemporary accounts
Historiographic consensus on Mali's economic and political significance

events.certainty_note: The hajj is attested in contemporary Egyptian sources (Al-Umari) and later Islamic chronicles. The date 1324 CE is well-established in Islamic and Western historiography, though some sources place it in 1323 CE. The scale and economic impact are documented.
cross_references: Relates to Mali Empire's rise (1230s onward)
Precedes Ibn Battuta's travels through Mali (1352-1353)

events.title: Execution of Templars and Suppression of the Knights Templar
events.description: King Philip IV of France executed the last Grand Master of the Knights Templar, Jacques de Molay, and other senior Templar leaders on 18 March 1314 (some sources date this to 1314, not 1324; see disconfirming evidence). The Templar Order, once a powerful military and financial institution, had been systematically dismantled since 1307 following accusations of heresy and financial impropriety. The suppression of the Templars marked the end of the Crusading military orders' dominance and reflected the rising power of centralized European monarchies over international religious institutions.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay (Grand Master of the Knights Templar)

sources.name: Contemporary French royal chronicles (e.g., Grandes Chroniques de France)

events.certainty_note: The execution of Jacques de Molay is well-documented, but the date is disputed. Most modern scholarship places it on 18 March 1314, not 1324. This entry is included here due to potential dating confusion in the research prompt; see disconfirming evidence.
cross_references: Follows from Templar arrests and trials (1307 onward)
Part of broader decline of Crusading movement

events.title: Zheng He's Early Naval Expeditions (Yuan Dynasty Context)
events.description: While Zheng He's famous treasure voyages began in 1405 under the Ming Dynasty, the Yuan Dynasty under Toghon Temür (Huizong) was actively engaged in maritime trade and naval expeditions in the Indian Ocean. In 1324, Yuan fleets were operating along established trade routes, and the Yuan court was consolidating control over maritime commerce. This period laid groundwork for later Ming naval dominance. However, specific documented expeditions for 1324 are sparse.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty) — compiled later
Historiographic consensus on Yuan maritime activity

events.certainty_note: Yuan maritime activity is documented, but specific events in 1324 are not well-attested. This entry reflects the broader era context rather than a confirmed discrete event.
cross_references: Part of Yuan Dynasty's maritime expansion

events.title: Osman I's Consolidation of Ottoman Power in Anatolia
events.description: Osman I (Osman Bey), founder of the Ottoman Empire, was consolidating his principality in northwestern Anatolia during the 1320s. By 1324, Osman had established control over significant territory and was beginning the military campaigns that would expand Ottoman holdings at the expense of the declining Byzantine Empire and neighboring Turkish beyliks. The Ottoman state would eventually become one of the world's largest empires, but in 1324 it was still a regional power in its formative stages.
key_figures: Osman I (Osman Bey, founder of the Ottoman Empire)
Orhan Bey (Osman's son, co-ruler and successor)

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (e.g., Aşıkpaşazade's Tevarih-i Al-i Osman) — written later

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the 1320s is well-established, but specific events in 1324 are not precisely dated in contemporary sources. Ottoman chronicles were written generations later and contain legendary elements.
cross_references: Contemporary with decline of Byzantine Empire

disconfirming_evidence: The execution of Jacques de Molay, often associated with 1324 in popular history, is actually dated to 18 March 1314 by modern scholarship. This is a common dating error. Additionally, while Mansa Musa's hajj is confirmed, some sources date it to 1323 CE rather than 1324 CE; the Islamic calendar conversion creates minor ambiguity. The specific events attributed to Osman I in 1324 are not precisely dated in contemporary sources; Ottoman chronicles are legendary in character and written centuries later. No major scientific, technological, or cultural breakthroughs are definitively attributed to 1324 in global records.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1324 is uneven. Islamic and West African sources (Al-Umari, later compilations) provide good coverage of Mali and the Hajj. European chronicles are abundant but often parochial (focused on Christendom). Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) are available but compiled retrospectively. Ottoman sources are legendary rather than contemporary. The record is biased toward literate, centralized states and toward Islamic and Christian regions; indigenous American, Pacific, and sub-Saharan African histories are underrepresented in surviving written sources. Archaeological evidence for this year is minimal.

graph_edges.note: Mansa Musa's hajj demonstrated Mali's wealth and consolidated its position as a major Islamic and trading power
Final suppression of the Knights Templar, following their arrest in 1307
Ottoman consolidation in 1324 was part of the long process leading to Byzantine collapse
